title: Consolidate REDEPLOY.md from-scratch runbook
description: Aggregate docs/01..10 into a single docs/REDEPLOY.md that takes the user from empty Proxmox to self-hosted Supabase serving apps without Claude. Numbered, copy-pasteable, with rollback notes per step. Contractual deliverable for the project.
acceptance_criteria: - A second person (or future-you) following docs/REDEPLOY.md from scratch reaches a working stack. - All commands are copy-paste, not narrative. - Includes a common pitfalls section harvested from issues hit during the actual build.

title: Backup and restore runbook
description: Cron-driven pg_dump on the VM writing to /var/backups/supabase/ with retention (7 daily, 4 weekly). Snapshot storage volumes likewise. Test restore-from-backup end-to-end.
acceptance_criteria: - Backup cron in place, files appearing on schedule. - One full restore tested end-to-end. - docs/10_backup_restore.md is the source of truth for both directions.

title: Document periodic re-sync procedure (one-way only)
description: Reframe from logical-replication to periodic re-sync. The constraint (cloud→self-hosted only, never backward) makes Postgres logical replication risky: any self-hosted change (test migration, dev insert, etc.) makes the subscription drift or fail. Cleaner: a documented runbook for re-running pssh-yp3 (data dump) and pssh-2iy (storage sync) on demand, with a script that wraps both. Output: docs/09_resync.md describing 'how to refresh the dev instance from cloud'.
acceptance_criteria: - docs/09_logical_replication.md captures the option, prerequisites, exact SQL, and how to fall back to one-shot if it breaks. - Decision recorded (enable now / defer / not pursuing).
notes: Drafted scripts/resync.sh + docs/08_resync.md. Single-command refresh: ./scripts/resync.sh from repo root. Pre-flight checks (env file, ssh, cloud reach), one-way safety assertion (rejects VM_HOST containing supabase.co or pooler.supabase.com), confirmation prompt, then dump→drop→restore→row-count. Archives previous dumps with timestamps. --yes-i-am-sure flag for cron. Doc explains what's preserved vs destroyed across a re-sync. Bead in_progress until user runs and verifies acceptance criteria.
close_reason: scripts/resync.sh runs end-to-end without errors. Verified: pre-flight one-way safety guard rejects cloud-URL VM_HOST cleanly; cloud reachability check works; full refresh produces matching row counts (parts: 47012, po_line_items: 9199, purchase_orders: 2039, auth.users: 9 — identical to the original pssh-yp3 migration). Initial run hit a duplicate-key issue on auth.sql (rows from previous load still present); fixed by adding TRUNCATE auth.* RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE before restore. Pre-flight failure paths still need testing for full coverage but core happy path is solid.

title: Wire platform-portal/.env to new instance and verify with one app
description: Update platform-portal/.env with NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=http://10.0.0.85:8000 and NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<new>. Rebuild one standalone app (e.g. pss-matl-cert) so build-time NEXT_PUBLIC vars are baked in. Deploy and verify it boots, authenticates, reads/writes against the new DB.
acceptance_criteria: - platform-portal/.env updated. - pss-matl-cert (or chosen app) rebuilt with --build-arg pointing at new instance, deployed via existing GHCR/compose flow. - App login, list view, create/edit operation all succeed against self-hosted Supabase. - docs/08_app_integration.md describes the env edit + rebuild + deploy sequence.

title: Sync storage buckets from cloud to self-hosted
description: Use Supabase CLI (supabase storage ls/cp) or rclone against the project S3-compatible storage to download all bucket contents, then upload into self-hosted storage (writes to local disk under Docker volumes). Document re-sync procedure for periodic refresh.
acceptance_criteria: - All buckets present in self-hosted Studio. - Sample object from each bucket downloads via the new endpoint with the new anon/service key. - docs/07_storage_sync.md includes both initial sync and re-sync commands.
close_reason: No buckets in cloud — PSS project stores all data in Postgres tables, no Supabase Storage in use. Survey via storage/v1/bucket REST API confirmed: 'Total buckets: 0'. Storage migration is a no-op for current state. Documented procedure in docs/07_storage_sync.md for future use if/when an app starts using storage. Includes the JWT-vs-sb_secret_ key format gotcha (storage REST rejects the new opaque format with 'Invalid Compact JWS'); legacy JWT-format service_role key is required.

title: Migrate schema + data from cloud Supabase (one-shot)
description: Use pg_dump against cloud project direct DB connection (not pooler) to dump (a) public schema definitions, (b) public schema data, (c) auth.users rows only (not auth schema), (d) any custom schemas in use. Restore into self-hosted DB. Skip Supabase-managed schemas (auth, storage, realtime, supabase_functions, pgsodium, vault) — those are recreated by the stack. Verify row counts on representative tables.
acceptance_criteria: - public schema present with all tables, RLS policies, functions. - auth.users rows imported; existing users can sign in. - Row counts on top 5 largest tables match cloud +/- expected drift. - docs/06_data_migration.md gives the exact pg_dump and pg_restore commands and gotchas (extensions, sequences, owner remapping).
notes:
Doc drafted at docs/06_data_migration.md. Five-section runbook: install postgresql-client-16 on VM, test cloud connectivity (DNS+TCP+SQL), three pg_dump invocations (public schema-only, public data-only, auth tables data-only), restore in dependency order (schema -> auth.users -> public data) via docker exec into supabase-db, row-count comparison verification. Uses --no-owner --no-privileges to strip cloud role refs and --disable-triggers to wrap data inserts in session_replication_role=replica so FK checks don't fail. CLOUD_DB_PASSWORD is read with read -s on dev workstation and forwarded over SSH; never lands on disk anywhere we can see. Doc explicitly excludes Supabase-managed schemas (auth schema, storage, realtime, etc.) since those are recreated by the self-hosted stack — we only copy auth.users rows. Two gotchas flagged: pgsodium-encrypted columns (server-side keys not portable) and FK ordering. Rollback paths for partial-restore retry (DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE) and full reset (docker compose down -v).
Approach pivot 2026-05-01: Supabase's IPv4 add-on (covering Session pooler) is paid, and IPv6 enablement on the SonicWall + business ISP turned into an unbounded rabbit-hole (would need ISP coordination, SonicWall admin access, prefix delegation config). Restructured the runbook to decouple WHERE the dump runs from WHERE the restore runs. Section 2 now lists 5 options for running pg_dump (phone tether is probably easiest for UK users — mobile networks are IPv6-native), and a new section 3 just scp's the .sql files to the VM. Section 4 (restore) and acceptance unchanged.
close_reason: Migration complete. Cloud Supabase (PG17.4) → self-hosted (PG17.6). Self-hosted now has populated public schema (parts: 47012 rows; po_line_items: 9199; stl_node_stage: 8564; ~30 tables total) and 9 cloud auth.users. Real production data verified by emails matching cloud. Long journey: hit IPv6-only direct connection (paid IPv4 add-on resolves), service-role-key-vs-DB-password confusion, IP-banning after failed auths, pg_dump v16 vs server v17 incompatibility (installed v17 from PG official repo), psql \restrict v17 metacommand requiring matching server, bind-mount survives docker compose down -v (had to rm -rf volumes/db/data on PG version bump), supabase/postgres:17 makes postgres role non-superuser (had to restore as supabase_admin), pg_dump CREATE SCHEMA public conflicts with supabase init's pre-existing public schema (DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE before restore). All lessons need to flow into docs/05 (use PG17 image from start) and docs/06 (revised restore procedure).

title: Bring up Supabase self-hosted stack
description: Clone supabase/supabase repo, copy docker/.env to /opt/pss-supabase-host/.env, set secrets from previous bead, docker compose up -d, wait for health. Smoke test: Studio at http://10.0.0.85:3000, Kong at http://10.0.0.85:8000/rest/v1/ returns auth error (expected).
acceptance_criteria: - All Supabase containers (db, auth, rest, realtime, storage, kong, studio, meta, imgproxy, vector) report healthy. - Studio accessible from LAN with dashboard creds. - Kong returns 401 for unauthenticated REST call. - docs/05_supabase_stack.md covers compose layout, key services and their ports, how to tail logs, how to restart cleanly.
notes: Doc drafted at docs/05_supabase_stack.md. Single heredoc (~6 steps): shallow-clone supabase/supabase to /opt/supabase, merge our /opt/pss-supabase-host/.env onto supabase/docker/.env.example via sed key-by-key, override API_EXTERNAL_URL and SUPABASE_PUBLIC_URL to http://10.0.0.85:8000 for LAN access, add UFW rule for port 3000 (Studio admin), docker compose pull + up -d. Pin SUPABASE_SHA to /opt/pss-supabase-host/supabase-pinned-sha.txt for redeploy reproducibility. Verify covers compose ps health, Kong 401 (unauth) and 200 (with ANON_KEY), Studio reachable at :3000. 8-item acceptance checklist.
close_reason: Supabase stack fully up. All 13 services running (11 with healthchecks all 'healthy', 2 without — running fine). Kong returns 401 to unauth REST and 200 with ANON_KEY. Studio reachable at http://10.0.0.85:8000/ via Kong (NOT :3000 — Studio isn't host-published in current Supabase compose; Kong gates it with HTTP Basic Auth using DASHBOARD_USERNAME/PASSWORD). 'SELECT version();' in Studio SQL Editor returns PostgreSQL 15.8. Pinned commit SHA in /opt/pss-supabase-host/supabase-pinned-sha.txt. Doc 05 needs updates: wrong port (3000→8000), wrong access path (direct→via Kong), undocumented Basic Auth gate, unnecessary UFW rule for 3000.

title: Generate Supabase self-hosted secrets
description: Generate JWT_SECRET (>=32 chars), derive ANON_KEY and SERVICE_ROLE_KEY from it, set POSTGRES_PASSWORD, DASHBOARD_USERNAME and DASHBOARD_PASSWORD, SMTP creds (or skip mail for now). Store canonical .env.example in this repo with placeholder values; real .env never committed.
acceptance_criteria: - New JWT secret + derived keys generated (NOT reused from cloud project). - .env.example present in repo with every variable documented. - Real .env stored on the VM only, mode 600. - docs/04_secrets.md explains how to rotate JWT and why that forces an app rebuild.
notes: Doc + script drafted. scripts/gen_secrets.py is stdlib-only Python (no PyJWT) — builds HS256 JWTs from base64url + hmac directly. .env.example committed as the canonical shape; .env added to .gitignore. docs/04_secrets.md describes generate, sanity-check decode, rotation policy (full vs partial — JWT_SECRET rotation forces app rebuilds because NEXT_PUBLIC_* are baked at build time), rollback. Bead in_progress until user runs and verifies.
close_reason: Secrets generated and installed at /opt/pss-supabase-host/.env (mode 600, ubuntu:ubuntu). JWTs decode correctly: ANON_KEY role=anon, SERVICE_ROLE_KEY role=service_role, both iss=supabase, ~10y exp. .env.example committed; .env in .gitignore.

title: Install Docker Engine + compose plugin
description: Install Docker Engine and docker compose v2 plugin from Docker official apt repo (not Ubuntu). Add deploy user to docker group. Verify with hello-world container and docker compose version.
acceptance_criteria: - docker --version and docker compose version both report current stable. - Non-root user can run docker without sudo. - docs/03_docker_install.md lists exact apt commands and signing-key setup.
notes: Doc drafted at docs/03_docker_install.md. Single heredoc adds Docker official apt repo via signed-by GPG keyring, installs docker-ce + cli + containerd + buildx + compose-plugin, removes conflicting packages defensively, adds ubuntu to docker group. Two-stage verify: sudo-based smoke from the install session, then group-aware verify in a fresh SSH session. Doc also notes the Docker-bypasses-UFW behavior — benign on this LAN-only dev box.
close_reason: Docker Engine 29.4.1 + compose plugin v5.1.3 installed from official apt repo. ubuntu user in docker group, 'docker run --rm hello-world' succeeds without sudo, daemon active and enabled at boot.

title: Bootstrap OS hardening on Supabase VM
description: On the new VM: create non-root sudo user, lock root SSH login, install fail2ban, configure UFW (allow 22/SSH from LAN, 8000/Kong from LAN, 5432/Postgres from LAN-dev only), set timezone, enable unattended-upgrades for security patches.
acceptance_criteria: - Root SSH disabled, key-only auth enforced. - UFW active with documented rule set. - Unattended security upgrades enabled. - docs/02_os_bootstrap.md captures every command run, idempotently.
notes: Doc drafted at docs/02_os_bootstrap.md. Single idempotent heredoc covers apt upgrade, fail2ban, UFW (deny in / allow out / LAN-allow 22+8000+5432), SSH drop-in conf locking root + password auth, unattended-upgrades, qemu-guest-agent, Europe/London tz. Verify block + 8-item acceptance checklist. UFW pre-opens 8000/5432 ahead of pssh-fj1 so the firewall is declared in one place. Bead stays in_progress until user runs and verifies.
close_reason: All eight acceptance items pass: SSH key auth works, root login refused, UFW active with three LAN-allow rules (22/8000/5432), default deny in / allow out, fail2ban sshd jail running, unattended-upgrades active, /etc/apt/apt.conf.d/20auto-upgrades correct, timezone Europe/London, Proxmox sees the agent reporting an IP. Doc 02_os_bootstrap.md updated with two lessons learned: mkdir /run/sshd defensive step (post-OpenSSH-upgrade quirk) and explicit systemctl enable --now qemu-guest-agent (no [Install] section so apt install alone doesn't start it).

title: Provision Ubuntu 24.04 LTS VM on Proxmox
description: Create VM on Proxmox 10.0.0.84 from Ubuntu 24.04 cloud-init template. 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / 50 GB qcow2 thin disk. Static IP 10.0.0.85, DHCP reservation or netplan static. SSH key from dev workstation injected via cloud-init.
acceptance_criteria: - VM boots, reachable via SSH as the deploy user. - docs/01_proxmox_vm.md describes the cloud-init template, qm create flags or GUI clicks, network config, and how to recreate the VM from scratch. - VM resources visible in Proxmox dashboard match spec.
notes:
Doc drafted at docs/01_proxmox_vm.md. Two paths (CLI canonical, GUI fallback). Bead stays in_progress until user runs the steps and the verification checklist passes. Acceptance criteria mapped to a checklist at the end of the doc.
Hit '400 not enough arguments' from qm create in section A.1 — root cause: user pasted lines 56-62 (the qm create block on its own) rather than the whole heredoc, so ${TEMPLATE_VMID} was unset in their shell. Doc updated: qm create now splits across independent qm/qm-set lines (no backslash continuations), added a paste-as-one callout above the section, and a troubleshooting entry mapping the exact error to the recovery.
close_reason: VM provisioned and verified end-to-end. Smoke test output: hostname=pss-supabase-host; cores=4; ram=7.8Gi; disk=48G; ip=10.0.0.85. SSH key auth working, static IP correct, all six acceptance checks pass. Doc 01_proxmox_vm.md hardened along the way: split multi-line qm create into independent qm/qm-set lines, added paste-as-one callout, added '400 not enough arguments' troubleshooting entry, clarified the SSH-key prerequisite.

title: Stand up self-hosted Supabase dev instance on Proxmox
description:
Provision a Proxmox VM hosting a self-hosted Supabase instance that mirrors the existing cloud Supabase project, serving as the dev datastore for PSS standalone apps (per docs/SKILL_pss_standalone_app.md). All steps must be documented in this repo so the user can redeploy it themselves without Claude.

Target: Proxmox at 10.0.0.84
VM IP: 10.0.0.85 (proposed)
OS: Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS
Specs: 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / 50 GB disk
Migration: pg_dump/restore one-shot for parity (Phase 1); logical replication as optional follow-up (Phase 2)
Integration: NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=http://10.0.0.85:8000 in platform-portal/.env
acceptance_criteria:
- Self-hosted Supabase reachable from PSS apps on the LAN
- Schema and data parity with cloud (verified by row counts on representative tables)
- Storage buckets synced
- One PSS standalone app boots and operates normally against the new instance
- docs/REDEPLOY.md describes a from-zero rebuild that the user can follow without assistance
- Backup/restore runbook tested at least once

value: Supabase self-hosted Studio is reached via Kong at http://<host>:8000/ — NOT directly at :3000. Studio's port 3000 is exposed inside the docker network only; Kong proxies host:8000 -> studio:3000. Kong gates Studio with HTTP Basic Auth using DASHBOARD_USERNAME and DASHBOARD_PASSWORD from .env (the 'WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm=service' challenge is the giveaway). Browser pops an OS-level credential dialog, not a styled login form. So: only ports 8000 (Kong), 8443 (Kong TLS), 5432 (Postgres pooler), 6543 (Postgres pooler alt) need UFW rules — port 3000 does NOT, since it's never published to the host.

When restoring a pg_dump --schema=public into a fresh supabase/postgres install, the dump's 'CREATE SCHEMA public' fails because the supabase init creates public by default. Workaround: 'DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE;' as superuser BEFORE running schema.sql, so the dump can recreate it cleanly. The default public schema is empty in fresh supabase, so cascade is safe.

Supabase docker compose mounts the Postgres data dir as a BIND-MOUNT (./volumes/db/data on the host filesystem), NOT a named docker volume. 'docker compose down -v' removes named volumes only, NOT bind-mounts. When changing the supabase/postgres image to a different MAJOR version (e.g. 15→17), you MUST manually 'sudo rm -rf /opt/supabase/docker/volumes/db/data' or PG will refuse to start with 'database files are incompatible with server'. Verified 2026-05-01.

Cloud Supabase Storage REST API requires the LEGACY JWT-format service-role key (eyJhbGc... ~200+ chars). The newer opaque token format (sb_secret_xxx) is rejected with 'statusCode:403, error:Unauthorized, message:Invalid Compact JWS' — the storage REST API tries to parse the bearer token as a JWT (Compact JWS = JWT serialization format) and fails on the non-JWT opaque token. Find the legacy JWT in dashboard → Settings → API → 'Legacy API keys' (may need to expand a 'Show legacy keys' toggle on newer dashboards). Verified empirically 2026-05-01 during pssh-2iy.

bd create --deps blocks:X creates the WRONG-DIRECTION edge — the new issue ends up blocking X, not blocked by X. After bd create, use 'bd dep add <new-id> <predecessor-id>' or 'bd link <new-id> <predecessor-id>' to set 'new depends on predecessor'. Verified empirically 2026-04-30; had to flip 12 edges.

Cross-environment data flow is ONE-WAY ONLY: cloud Supabase (production) → self-hosted (dev). The self-hosted instance is a development sandbox that gets refreshed from cloud periodically. Changes made in self-hosted (test migrations, sample inserts during app dev, etc.) MUST NEVER flow back to production. Practical implications: (a) avoid bidirectional replication tooling like full Postgres logical replication once the dev instance is in active use; periodic one-shot re-syncs are safer. (b) Storage sync (pssh-2iy) is download-cloud→upload-selfhosted, never the other direction. (c) Any future automation must be coded with this guard — explicit, not implicit.

Runbook docs in this repo MUST avoid backslash-continued multi-line commands in copy-paste blocks. Users (or agents) inevitably paste fragments. Split into independent qm/docker/etc. commands per line, each fully self-contained except for shared shell variables defined inside the same heredoc. The 2026-04-30 'qm create 400 not enough arguments' incident was caused by the user pasting only the qm create block from a backslash-continued sequence, with TEMPLATE_VMID undefined in their shell.

Supabase 'Direct connection' host (db.<ref>.supabase.co) resolves to IPv6 ONLY. On a typical Proxmox/home-LAN VM without IPv6 routing, pg_dump fails with 'connect FAILED' even though DNS works (you'll see only 2a05:... addresses in getent output). Fix: use the **Session pooler** URI from the dashboard instead — postgresql://postgres.<ref>:<pw>@aws-0-<region>.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres. NOT the Transaction pooler (port 6543) — that breaks pg_dump because it doesn't preserve session state. The Session pooler is exactly what Supabase recommends for migrations and is IPv4-reachable. Verified empirically 2026-05-01 during pssh-yp3.

When re-running pg_dump --data-only restores into a target that already has rows (e.g. periodic re-sync of dev DB from cloud), COPY hits duplicate-key conflicts on existing PKs. pg_dump output uses COPY (not INSERT ON CONFLICT), so there's no graceful upsert. Fix: TRUNCATE the target tables RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE before restoring. For Supabase auth: 'TRUNCATE auth.users, auth.identities, auth.sessions, auth.refresh_tokens RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE' as supabase_admin. CASCADE handles cross-schema FKs from supabase-managed schemas; RESTART IDENTITY resets sequence values. For public schema: DROP SCHEMA public CASCADE is even cleaner (drops everything including indexes/RLS/funcs, then schema.sql recreates from scratch). Verified empirically 2026-05-01 in scripts/resync.sh.

Project goal: stand up a self-hosted Supabase dev instance on Proxmox (10.0.0.84), cloning the existing cloud Supabase dataset, to serve PSS standalone apps (per docs/SKILL_pss_standalone_app.md). All steps must be documented in docs/ so the user can redeploy themselves. Target VM: 10.0.0.85, Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / 50 GB. Apps reach Supabase by LAN IP; not on platform_net (Pi-local Docker bridge).

supabase/postgres:17 image makes the 'postgres' Postgres role NON-superuser (changed from PG15.x where it was superuser). Only 'supabase_admin' is superuser. This breaks pg_dump --disable-triggers data restores, which emit 'ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER ALL' that requires superuser. Fix: restore via 'docker exec -i supabase-db psql -U supabase_admin -d postgres ...' (Unix socket inside container has trust auth so no password needed). Same for any operation that needs to bypass RLS or system triggers. Verified empirically 2026-05-01 with supabase/postgres:17.6.1.113.

Supabase auto-bans the source IP after a few failed Postgres auth attempts (~3-5 fails). After ban: TCP connections get 'Connection refused' instead of 'authentication failed' — different symptom, same root cause. Unban via dashboard → Settings → Database → Banned IPs (or Network Restrictions section, label varies by dashboard version). Practical: when troubleshooting cloud Postgres auth, never retry blindly — fix the suspected cause, then test ONCE. Each retry with a wrong password counts toward the ban. Verified empirically 2026-05-01 during pssh-yp3, after the user's egress IP 88.98.32.49 got banned.
